title: What Is Required for Eternal Life?
teaching:
This week, read John 16-21 in your own Bible. As you read, watch how John answers the question: What is required for eternal life? Look for the work of the Spirit, the prayer of Jesus, the cross, the resurrection, the response of Thomas, and the restoration of Peter.

The anchor passage is John 20:26-31. Near the end of the Gospel, John tells readers why he wrote: so that they may believe Jesus is the Christ, the Son of God, and have life in his name. Eternal life is not earned by religious effort, family background, moral improvement, or perfect understanding. It is received by believing in Jesus as he is revealed.

John 16 shows that the Spirit exposes sin, righteousness, and judgment, and guides Jesus' followers into truth. John 17 shows Jesus praying for those the Father has given him. John 18-19 shows Jesus rejected, tried, crucified, and buried. John presents the cross not as an accident, but as the place where Jesus completes the work given to him.

Then John 20 turns from death to witness. Mary Magdalene, the disciples, and Thomas encounter the risen Jesus. Thomas moves from doubt to confession, not because he solved every mystery, but because he met the risen Lord. John 21 then shows that failure is not the end for those who belong to Jesus. Peter is restored and called again to follow.

As you read, ask: What does John invite me to believe about Jesus, and what would it mean to entrust myself to him?
outside_the_box: In John, eternal life is not only endless existence after death. It is life that comes from knowing God through Jesus. That life begins now and continues beyond death. This helps us avoid two mistakes. We do not reduce salvation to a future destination only. We also do not reduce it to present spiritual improvement. Eternal life is personal, relational, and centered on the crucified and risen Son.

reflection_questions.text: In John 16-21, what does Jesus accomplish that people could never accomplish for themselves?
What do you notice about the different responses to the risen Jesus in John 20?
How does Peter's restoration in John 21 help you understand grace after failure?
What is the difference between knowing facts about Jesus and believing in Jesus?

live_it_out: This week, write a simple personal response to Jesus. Use three headings: *What I see*, *What I believe*, and *What I still struggle with*. Be honest. Bring one sentence from your response to the final huddle meeting.
prayer_prompt: Ask the risen Jesus to lead you from observation to trust. Thank him for completing the work of salvation and ask for grace to follow him honestly, even with remaining questions.

huddle_discussion_prompts.text: Begin by inviting each person to share one scene from John 16-21 that felt most important to them.
Discuss q04. Help the group distinguish mere agreement, emotional pressure, and genuine trust in Jesus.
Invite participants to share from q03. How does Jesus deal with failure in John 21?
Close the series by inviting each person to share one sentence from the live_it_out practice, without forcing anyone to say more than they are ready to say.

leader_notes: This final lesson may bring people to a point of decision, but do not manipulate the moment. John's own purpose is clear: readers are invited to believe and have life in Jesus. Explain faith as personal trust in the crucified and risen Christ, not as perfect knowledge or a religious performance. Be ready for a range of responses: joyful faith, cautious openness, honest doubt, or quiet resistance. Honor honesty while clearly naming the invitation. Peter's restoration can help those ashamed of failure. End with prayer and, if appropriate, offer follow-up conversation for anyone who wants to respond to Jesus more personally.
